methods.name: Stage-Progression

methods.name: Peer-Median

methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Phasecraft is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Montanaro, Cubitt, and Morton that has raised $25M to date. The quantum computing sector contains 11 total comparable companies—3 public (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private peers including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Stage-progression analysis is the applicable valuation method here. Using total capital raised ($25M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $50M. This method captures the anticipated value creation and dilution economics characteristic of early-stage frontier-tech financings.

Peer-median benchmarking is not applicable. While seven same-sector peers exist in the quantum computing universe, the narrow filter—requiring same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier comparables—returned fewer than three datapoints, making median-based triangulation unreliable. Broadening to all-sector peers would introduce material methodological error.

The valuation range supported by this analysis is $42M to $57M, with a median point of $50M. This reflects a single credible methodology and carries material uncertainty regarding which quantum modality and go-to-market approach Phasecraft pursues relative to the heterogeneous peer set. The limited comparable universe at Phasecraft's specific stage and capitalization tier remains the binding constraint on precision.
